data.title: If You Invested $1000 In T-Mobile US Stock 10 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
T-Mobile US (NASDAQ:TMUS) has outperformed the market over the past 10 years by 6.29% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 18.89%. Currently, T-Mobile US has a market capitalization of $255.48 billion.

Buying $1000 In TMUS: If an investor had bought $1000 of TMUS stock 10 years ago, it would be worth $5,577.64 today based on a price of $227.01 for TMUS at the time of writing.

T-Mobile US's Performance Over Last 10 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.

data.title: Gold Hits $4,270, Bitcoin Plunges Below $110,000: What's Moving Markets Thursday?
data.description:
In an otherwise cautious session on Wall Street, gold and silver stole the spotlight once again, with both precious metals pushing to fresh record highs — gold touching $4,270 per ounce and silver climbing to $53.90.

• GDX is reaching significant price levels. See what is driving the movement here.

Thursday's 1.5% gain in gold positions it to close its 14th positive session out of the past 16, extending its year-to-date rally to 63%, putting it well on track for the best annual performance since 1979.

Gold miners are riding the wave higher. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSE:GDX) rose another 4% Thursday, after gaining 3.9% the day before. That brings the fund's weekly gain to 12%, while its year-to-date return now sits at a staggering 150%, outperforming every other equity industry tracked on Wall Street.

By comparison, the Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (NYSE:AIQ) — a popular vehicle for AI-focused names — is up just 30% year-to-date.

Fueling Thursday’s rally in precious metals were dovish comments from Federal Reserve Gov. Christopher Waller, who expressed support for another rate cut at the upcoming Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting.

"Based on all of the data we have on the labor market, I believe that the FOMC should reduce the policy rate another 25 basis points," Waller said, adding that policy should move toward a "neutral level," which he estimates is 100 to 125 basis points lower than current levels.

The S&P 500, Nasdaq 100 and Dow Jones Industrial Average all hovered near unchanged levels Thursday, while the Russell 2000 slipped 1%, reflecting weakness in small caps.

Semiconductor stocks stood out on the upside after Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (NYSE:TSM) beat earnings forecasts and delivered upbeat guidance. Micron Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) jumped nearly 6%, while NVIDIA Corp. (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) each added around 1%.

Meanwhile, the crypto market turned lower again. Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) fell more than 2% to $108,000, marking its third consecutive daily loss and bringing it close to its lowest level since July. Ethereum (CRYPTO: ETH) dropped 2.7%, while Solana (CRYPTO: SOL) plunged nearly 4%.

Thursday’s Performance In Major U.S. Indices, ETFs

According to Benzinga Pro data:

  • The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSE:VOO) flattened at $610.87.
  • The SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average (NYSE:DIA) inched 0.1% lower to $462.05.
  • The tech-heavy Invesco QQQ Trust Series (NASDAQ:QQQ) edged 0.2% higher to $603.54.
  • The iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSE:IWM) fell 1% to $247.83.
  • The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLRE) outperformed, up 0.5%; the Financials Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLF) lagged, down 1.3%.

Thursday’s Earnings Movers

Stocks moving on earnings reports included:

  • Charles Schwab Corporation (NYSE:SCHW) up 0.9%
  • Marsh & McLennan Companies, Inc. (NYSE:MMC) down 5.6%
  • Bank of New York Mellon Corp (NYSE:BK) up 0.4%
  • U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) up 0.5%
  • Kinder Morgan, Inc. (NYSE:KMI) down 0.9%
  • Travelers Companies, Inc. (NYSE:TRV) down 2.6%
  • United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UAL) down 9.4%
  • M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB) down 2.6%
  • KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) down 3.7%
  • Snap-on Incorporated (NYSE:SNA) up 4.3%
  • J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (NASDAQ:JBHT) up 19.7%
  • Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (NYSE:REXR) up 1.9%
  • First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (NYSE:FR) up 1.4%
  • Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. (NASDAQ:PNFP) down 2.7%
  • MarketAxess Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:MKTX) down 0.8%
  • Synovus Financial Corp. (NYSE:SNV) down 2.7%

Stocks slated to report after the close include CSX Corp. (NASDAQ:CSX), Interactive Brokers Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:IBKR), Watsco, Inc. (NYSE:WSO) and MarketAxess Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:MKTX).

data.title: Lennox Expands HVAC Portfolio With $550 Million NSI Division Purchase
data.description:
Lennox International, Inc. (NYSE:LII) announced Thursday that it has completed the purchase of the HVAC Division of NSI Industries from Sentinel Capital Partners in a cash transaction valued at approximately $550 million.

The deal includes the well-known Duro Dyne and Supco brands, marking a significant expansion of Lennox’s heating, ventilation, and air conditioning portfolio.

With this divestiture, NSI will pivot to a pure-play electrical manufacturing business, focusing on core brands such as Bridgeport, Polaris, and Tork.

Also Read: Taiwan Semiconductor Lifts Outlook After Blowout Quarter, CEO Says AI Signals From Clients Are ‘Very Strong‘

The firm plans to strengthen its position as a supplier of high-quality electrical components that drive consistent, replacement-based demand in industrial and commercial markets.

The acquisition strengthens Lennox’s presence in the HVAC components sector by adding two established brands with a strong footprint in the North American market.

Sentinel partner John Van Sickle called the transaction an “excellent strategic fit,” emphasizing that it allows NSI to return to its core electrical roots while giving Lennox a growth platform within HVAC manufacturing and distribution.

NSI’s HVAC President John MacQuarrie expressed enthusiasm about joining Lennox, saying that the focus will remain on operational excellence and product innovation to deliver more value to customers as part of Lennox’s broader network.

Sentinel Capital Partners, known for investing in midmarket industrial and manufacturing companies, has a track record of transforming sector leaders.

Its portfolio has included firms such as ECM Industries, Power Products, and Chromalox, all of which specialize in electrical, control, and industrial systems.

The sale of NSI’s HVAC division continues Sentinel’s strategy of repositioning assets to maximize long-term value.

The move comes amid consolidation trends in the HVAC industry, as companies seek scale to meet demand for energy-efficient systems and supply chain resilience.

Price Action: LII shares were trading higher by 0.05% to $527.25 at last check Thursday.

  • Nvidia, Marvell, Broadcom Bounce Back After ASML Earnings Boost Investor Confidence

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: 10 Information Technology Stocks Whale Activity In Today's Session
data.description:
This whale alert can help traders discover the next big trading opportunities.

Whales are entities with large sums of money and we track their transactions here at Benzinga on our options activity scanner.

Traders often look for circumstances when the market estimation of an option diverges away from its normal worth. Abnormal amounts of trading activity could push option prices to hyperbolic or underperforming levels.

Below are some instances of options activity happening in the Information Technology sector:

Explanation

These itemized elaborations have been created using the accompanying table.

• Regarding AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), we observe a call option sweep with bearish sentiment. It expires in 1 day(s) on October 17, 2025. Parties traded 120 contract(s) at a $240.00 strike. This particular call needed to be split into 12 different trades to become filled. The total cost received by the writing party (or parties) was $40.2K, with a price of $335.0 per contract. There were 28244 open contracts at this strike prior to today, and today 82630 contract(s) were bought and sold.

• For ORCL (NYSE:ORCL), we notice a call option sweep that happens to be bullish, expiring in 1 day(s) on October 17, 2025. This event was a transfer of 452 contract(s) at a $340.00 strike. This particular call needed to be split into 5 different trades to become filled. The total cost received by the writing party (or parties) was $64.7K, with a price of $140.0 per contract. This particular put needed to be split into 24 different trades to become filled. The total cost received by the writing party (or parties) was $110.4K, with a price of $272.0 per contract. There were 21598 open contracts at this strike prior to today, and today 3850 contract(s) were bought and sold.

There were 5611 open contracts at this strike prior to today, and today 2104 contract(s) were bought and sold.

Options Alert Terminology
- Call Contracts: The right to buy shares as indicated in the contract.
- Put Contracts: The right to sell shares as indicated in the contract.
- Expiration Date: When the contract expires. One must act on the contract by this date if one wants to use it.
- Premium/Option Price: The price of the contract.

For more information, visit our Guide to Understanding Options Alerts or read more news on unusual options activity.

data.title: Under the Radar: How to Find the Market's Hidden Gems
data.description:
In every market cycle, a small number of companies quietly strengthen their balance sheets, improve profitability, and reinvest for growth — often before Wall Street takes notice. For disciplined investors, these are the real opportunities: small, financially sound businesses trading at valuations that still leave room for error.

Two of the most reliable tools for finding these companies are the Piotroski F-Score and the Altman Z-Score. When used together, they highlight small-cap stocks that combine financial improvement with balance-sheet safety — the essence of growth with a margin of safety.

Why Small-Cap Growth Stocks Matter

Small-cap stocks — generally companies with market capitalizations below $2 billion — have historically offered higher long-term returns than their large-cap counterparts. The reason lies in the mathematics of growth and the psychology of markets.

Smaller firms can expand earnings much faster than mature companies because they're starting from a lower base. They can double sales by adding one new product line or opening a few new markets. Their management teams tend to be entrepreneurial and agile, responding more quickly to economic or technological shifts. And since many operate in underfollowed or niche industries, their shares often remain mispriced for long stretches, allowing investors to buy growth at value prices.

Academic studies, including those by Eugene Fama and Kenneth French, have consistently found a "small-cap premium" over long time horizons. But that premium isn't free — it comes with volatility. Small-caps are more sensitive to credit cycles, supply shocks, and investor sentiment. That's why blending quantitative measures of quality and solvency — like the F-Score and Z-Score — is so powerful. These tools help investors separate the durable growers from the speculative pretenders.

When investors focus on financially strong small-cap growth companies, the long-term payoff can be extraordinary. Many of the market's legendary winners — from early-stage industrial suppliers to fintech innovators — began as small companies with improving fundamentals and clean balance sheets. Owning a portfolio of those before they are "discovered" is where real wealth is created.

The Piotroski F-Score: History and Purpose

The Piotroski F-Score, created in 2000 by accounting professor Joseph D. Piotroski at the University of Chicago, was designed to separate the strong value stocks from the value traps. His seminal study, published in the Journal of Accounting Research, showed that within the cheapest decile of the market by price-to-book, companies with high F-Scores outperformed low-scoring peers by more than 7.5% per year.

The F-Score is built on nine accounting signals that measure profitability, leverage, liquidity, and operational efficiency. Each positive result earns one point. Firms scoring 7–9 show robust and improving fundamentals; those scoring 0–3 typically face declining performance or distress.

Piotroski's insight was behavioral as much as financial: investors tend to lump all "cheap" stocks together, ignoring those quietly turning around. The F-Score uncovers those hidden recoveries — a hallmark of the small-cap sweet spot.

The Altman Z-Score: The Original Credit Safety Gauge

The Altman Z-Score, introduced by Edward Altman in 1968, is one of the earliest quantitative models of credit risk. Altman analyzed dozens of financial ratios across hundreds of companies and distilled them into a five-variable formula that accurately predicted bankruptcy risk.

Scores above 3.0 signal low risk of financial distress; those below 1.8 warn of trouble ahead. The Z-Score remains widely used today by lenders, credit analysts, and investors as a shorthand measure of balance-sheet safety.

When paired with the F-Score, it creates a complete picture: one measures financial improvement, the other measures financial durability. The result is a disciplined screen that identifies small-cap companies growing from a position of strength rather than speculation.

Fortunately, we have saved you the time and trouble of digging though the financial statements and completing score sheets by hand for these indicators. You can know run a simple quick screen in Benzinga Pro that gives you a list of small fast-growing companies with fortress financial statements in just seconds.

Three Examples of Growth with a Margin of Safety

Oil-Dri Corp. of America (NYSE:ODC)

Founded in 1941, Oil-Dri is a family-controlled producer of absorbent clay products used in consumer, industrial, and agricultural applications. Its most recognizable brand, Cat's Pride, remains a household staple. Over decades, Oil-Dri has evolved into a steady, cash-generating business with no net debt, consistent free cash flow, and 21 consecutive years of dividend increases.

The company's F-Score is near the maximum, reflecting strong profitability, positive cash flow, and improving returns on assets. Its Z-Score exceeds 3, confirming financial safety. Despite its conservative nature, ODC trades around tangible book value and roughly 10× forward earnings, providing both income and upside as cash flow compounds.

Rush Street Interactive (NYSE:RSI)

Rush Street Interactive, headquartered in Chicago, operates the BetRivers and PlaySugarHouse brands in online gaming and sports betting across North America and Latin America. Founded in 2012 and public since 2020, RSI distinguishes itself from its peers through disciplined growth and a strong balance sheet.

While competitors burn cash on customer acquisition, RSI has reduced marketing intensity and turned adjusted EBITDA positive in multiple quarters. Its Piotroski F-Score reflects rising margins, growing cash flow, and reduced leverage, while its Altman Z-Score above 3 underscores liquidity strength and minimal debt. Trading at a small fraction of its 2021 highs, RSI offers exposure to the digital gaming trend — but with fundamentals strong enough to weather market cycles.

ACI Worldwide (NASDAQ:ACIW)

ACI Worldwide, founded in 1975 and based in Miami, builds software that enables real-time digital payments and fraud prevention for banks, merchants, and processors worldwide. It's transitioning toward recurring, subscription-based revenue, driving higher margins and cash flow predictability.

With recurring revenue exceeding 80%, solid cash generation, and controlled leverage, ACIW earns a strong F-Score and a Z-Score comfortably above 3. Despite steady revenue growth and a large installed customer base, it trades at a valuation discount to larger fintech peers. For investors seeking growth in global payment infrastructure without overpaying, ACIW fits perfectly within the "growth with safety" framework.

The Takeaway

The Altman Z-Score and Piotroski F-Score are timeless tools that help investors stay focused on fundamentals amid the noise. They are particularly powerful in the small-cap segment, where information is scarce, volatility is high, and the rewards for disciplined analysis are greatest.

ODC, RSI, and ACIW each show the traits we look for — improving profitability, prudent balance-sheet management, and the ability to self-fund growth. For long-term investors, these are not just "cheap" stocks — they are financially improving businesses with sustainable growth engines, capable of compounding value over many years.

In a market that constantly shifts between greed and fear, this combination — small-cap agility, earnings momentum, and balance-sheet strength — remains one of the few strategies that has consistently delivered growth with a margin of safety.
